title: Willow bird feeder
subtitle: Small randed willow basket for garden bird seed
excerpt: A randed willow basket sized for the garden bird table. The base is a square slath, the sides are randed in single weave, and the rim is a three-rod wale. A first pass at randed willow basketry that yields a useful piece for the garden.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management on willow basketry. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for the randing and waling sequence.

glossaryTerms.definition: A willow basketry weave in which a single rod is woven over and under alternate stakes around the body of the basket.
A tight basketry weave in which three or four rods are woven together to strengthen a transition point such as the base of the sides or the rim.
The starting cross of stakes that forms the base of a square or round willow basket.
Thin flexible willow rods used to weave around the upright stakes.
The thicker upright willow rods that form the structural framework of the basket.

content.text: A small willow basket for the bird table: a 15 cm square base, 12 cm tall, with a wide rim for spreading seed and a slight inward curve at the top to keep light seed from blowing out. The weave is single
randing
, the simplest willow weave: one weaver over one stake, under the next, repeating around. Two rows of three-rod
waling
hold the structure at the base and at the rim. This is a first willow project; the weave forgives uneven tension and the small size keeps the rod count manageable.
The willow
Buff or steamed brown willow rods, 1.2 metres long: 8 thick rods for the
slath
base, 16 medium rods for the side
stakes
, and roughly 40 thin rods for
weavers

and waling. Buff is willow stripped of bark by boiling and easy for a first basket; steamed brown keeps the bark on and is more weather-resistant for outdoor use. Soak all the rods in cold water for 1 day before working: 1.2 metre rods take 24 hours; thicker rods up to 48 hours.
Tools

Sharp secateurs for trimming and ending rods, a bodkin (a thick steel awl) for opening the weave to tuck in rod ends, and a soaking tank long enough to lay the rods flat. A bath or a long trough works; a length of guttering with end caps is the basket-maker's traditional soak. Soft cotton string for tying the slath cross.
Method

Slit the slath. Split 4 of the 8 thick rods in half lengthwise with the bodkin to make 8 half-rods. Lay 4 half-rods parallel, 3 cm apart, on the bench. Across them, lay the other 4 half-rods at right angles. Tie a temporary cross with the cotton string. The result is a 15 cm by 15 cm cross of 4 stakes by 4 stakes.

Pair-weave around the slath cross. Take 2 thin weavers and bend them at the middle around one of the arms of the cross. Weave one over, one under as you go around the cross, switching the weavers' positions each time. Two rows lock the cross together; remove the string.

Open out the stakes. After the pair weave, the 4 stakes on each arm need to spread apart. Use the bodkin to lever them gently into an evenly spaced rectangular base, with stakes radiating outwards from the cross to form a 15 cm square.

Continue randing the base. Single rand: one weaver over one stake, under the next, around the perimeter. Add a new weaver when the old one runs out by butting the ends together inside the basket. Work until the base is full, 6 to 8 rows of weave.

Stake up the sides. Trim the base ends of the 16 side stakes to a long flat point. Push one stake down into the base weave alongside each base stake with the bodkin. The new stakes stand upright and become the framework of the sides.

Three-rod wale at the base of the sides. Three weavers worked together (each in turn going over two and under one stake) lock the upright stakes at the base before the side randing starts. Two rows of three-rod wale make the transition firm and even.
Rand the sides. Single rand around the uprights for 10 cm; the weave climbs steadily up the sides. Keep the tension even: too tight and the sides bow inwards, too loose and the weave gapes.
Finish the rim with two rows of three-rod wale, matching the base. Trim the weaver ends inside the rim.

Trim and turn the stakes to make a border. Soak the stakes briefly if they have stiffened: bend each in turn down across two stakes and tuck the end behind the third using the bodkin. The traditional 3-rod border seals the rim and looks like a woven plait.
Trim all loose ends with the secateurs. Cut them flush with the inside of the weave. The basket is finished.
Finishing

No finish needed for outdoor use; willow weathers to silver-grey in sun and rain. For a glossier indoor look, brush with raw linseed oil and let dry on a paper-lined shelf for 1 week. The bird feeder hangs from the four corners with cotton string tied to the top rim wale; alternatively, sit it on a low post under a roof for shelter.
Variations
Round base instead of square: use 6 thick rods slit at the centres to form an 8-armed cross. The round version weaves slightly faster but is harder to mount on a flat post.
Deeper sides: rand for 16 cm instead of 10 cm for a basket that holds enough seed for a week. The taller form is also good for table fruit indoors.
Steamed brown willow throughout for a rustic look: the bark gives a darker, more weather-resistant finish. The work is the same; the weavers are slightly less flexible.
Care

Empty and rinse the basket once a month with a hose. Bird seed crumbs left damp inside the weave will mould. Hang to dry overnight. The basket lasts 2 years outdoors before the weave loosens; longer if brought in over winter.

items.symptom: Slath cross will not lie flat on the bench
items.cause: Stakes not properly opened and spaced after the pair weave
items.fix: Lever each pair of base stakes outwards with the bodkin until they radiate evenly. The cross only flattens when the angles between adjacent stakes are roughly equal.

items.symptom: Rods snap when bent for the border
items.cause: Stakes too dry; soak time was not long enough or they have dried since soaking
items.fix: Wrap the upper part of each stake in a damp cloth for 30 minutes and try again. Willow loses flexibility within 1 hour of leaving the soak.

items.symptom: Sides bow inwards as you weave
items.cause: Weave tension too tight; stakes pulled in
items.fix: Loosen the tension on the next row. Push each stake outwards gently with the bodkin to restore the shape, then continue with looser weaving.

items.symptom: Weavers fail to butt cleanly inside the basket
items.cause: Both rod ends cut at the same angle facing outwards
items.fix: Cut new rod butts with the angle facing inwards. The butt end of the new weaver should sit alongside the tip end of the previous weaver, both pointing into the basket interior.
